title: Game Difficulty Selector
description: Create a game difficulty selector using if-else if-else chains!
instructions:
Write a program that selects game difficulty based on a level number:

1. Create an int variable called 'level' and set it to any number (try 1, 2, 3, or something else)
2. Use if-else if-else to display:
   - If level == 1: 'Easy mode selected'
   - Else if level == 2: 'Normal mode selected'
   - Else if level == 3: 'Hard mode selected'
   - Else: 'Invalid level! Please choose 1, 2, or 3.'

Test your code by changing the level value to make sure all paths work!

hints.text: Start with: if (level == 1) { Console.WriteLine(...); }. Remember to use == for comparison, not =!
Chain conditions together: if (...) { } else if (...) { } else if (...) { } else { }
Common mistake: Using = instead of ==. level = 1 ASSIGNS 1, level == 1 COMPARES!
Remember: only ONE block runs! If level is 2, only the second message displays.
The else must be LAST. It catches everything that didn't match above.

commonMistakes.consequence: level = 1 changes the value of level! Use == to compare.
commonMistakes.correction: Always use == when checking if two values are equal.

commonMistakes.consequence: If checking ranges, put most specific first (e.g., >= 90 before >= 80).
commonMistakes.correction: Order conditions from most specific to least specific.

commonMistakes.consequence: Beginners sometimes expect multiple messages to print.
commonMistakes.correction: Only the FIRST matching condition executes!
